BRD Document Content:
Business Requirements Document (BRD)
Core Banking Domain Data & Functional Requirements
1. Document Purpose
This BRD outlines the business requirements for the Core Banking domain covering customer onboarding, account lifecycle management, transactions processing, account balances, fees & charges, KYC compliance, and GL postings. The document provides a unified business view leveraging the supplied Core Banking ER diagram to ensure standardized data, seamless processing, and accurate financial reporting.
2. Business Background
Banks operate critical core systems to handle customer information, accounts, balances, transactions, fees, and ledger postings. Current systems often involve multiple legacy modules, manual reconciliations, inconsistent account status updates, and delays in GL posting visibility. A unified and governed data/functional view is essential for operational efficiency, regulatory compliance, and audit readiness.
3. Business Objectives
Establish a standardized Core Banking data model aligned with the bank's operational workflow.
Centralize customer, account, balance, transaction, fee, and GL data.
Ensure accurate, real-time and historical visibility of all customer and account activities.
Support regulatory, financial, and reconciliation reporting.
Reduce manual dependencies and improve process transparency.
4. Scope
In Scope:
Customer master & contact management
Account creation, modification, status lifecycle tracking
Capturing and storing financial transactions
Maintaining ledger and available balances
Fee computation and fee assessment
KYC profiling and periodic review tracking
General Ledger (GL) postings derived from transactions and fees
Out of Scope:
Loan origination workflows
Card systems
Wealth management or investment products
CRM segmentation logic beyond core identifiers
Treasury, market risk, or payments switching
5. Stakeholders
Retail Banking Operations
Customer Service & Branch Users
Finance & GL/Accounting Teams
Compliance & KYC Teams
Risk & Monitoring Teams
Data Engineering / Core Banking IT
Audit & Regulatory Reporting
6. Current State Challenges
Customer data inconsistencies across channels
Multiple representations of account status without event history
Manual KYC review tracking
Lack of unified view of transactions and balances
Delayed fee and GL posting visibility
High dependency on manual reconciliations
7. Future State Requirements
A unified data store linking customers, accounts, KYC, balances, transactions, and GL entries.
Event-driven account status lifecycle tracking.
Automated generation of GL postings for all monetary transactions and fee charges.
Real-time or near real-time balance updates.
Standardized customer and account attributes.
Full audit trail for all monetary and non-monetary activities.
8. Functional Requirements
8.1 Customer & KYC
System shall store core customer attributes including name, gender, DOB, and segment.
System shall store multiple customer contact details including phone, email, and address.
System shall maintain KYC profile including status, risk rating, last review and next review date.
System shall restrict customer onboarding unless minimum KYC criteria are met.
8.2 Account Management
System shall support creation of multiple account types (savings, current, deposit, etc.).
System shall track account currency, status, and ownership.
System shall store full account lifecycle status events (open, freeze, suspend, close, etc.).
System shall not allow transactions on closed or restricted accounts.
8.3 Transactions Processing
System shall store every transaction with timestamp, type, amount, and channel.
System shall support credit, debit, transfer, and adjustment transactions.
System shall update available balance and ledger balance in real time.
Each financial transaction shall create corresponding GL postings.
8.4 Balance Management
System shall maintain ledger and available balances for each account.
System shall maintain a timestamp for each balance snapshot.
System shall prevent transactions that exceed available balance where applicable.
8.5 Fees & Charges
System shall store fee definitions and transaction-level fee charges.
System shall apply fees based on fee type (maintenance, penalty, service fee, etc.).
System shall generate GL postings for all fee charges.
8.6 GL Posting & Accounting
System shall maintain mapping between transaction types and GL codes.
Every transaction and fee charge shall produce debit/credit GL entries.
System shall prevent unbalanced GL postings.
9. Reporting Requirements
Customer 360 view including customer profile, contact, KYC, and accounts.
Account 360 view including balance, status, transactions, and fees.
Account lifecycle status events reporting.
Daily transaction summaries by channel, type, and amount.
Fee revenue reporting by fee type and account.
GL trial balance alignment and exception monitoring.
KYC due-for-review reporting.
10. Data Requirements (Based on ER Diagram)
CUSTOMER: customer_id (PK), first_name, last_name, gender, date_of_birth, segment
CUSTOMER_CONTACT: customer_id (FK), mobile_number, email_id, address, city, country
ACCOUNT: account_id (PK), customer_id (FK), account_type, currency, account_status
ACCOUNT_STATUS_EVENT: status_event_id (PK), account_id (FK), old_status, new_status, change_timestamp
ACCOUNT_BALANCE: balance_id (PK), account_id (FK), as_of_timestamp, ledger_balance, available_balance
TRANSACTION: transaction_id (PK), account_id (FK), timestamp, type, amount, channel
FEE_CHARGE: fee_id (PK), account_id (FK), fee_type, fee_amount, assessment_date
KYC_PROFILE: kyc_id (PK), customer_id (FK), kyc_status, risk_rating, last_review_date, next_due_date
GL_ACCOUNT: gl_account_code (PK), gl_account_name, gl_type
GL_POSTING: gl_posting_id (PK), transaction_id (FK), gl_account_code (FK), dc_indicator, amount
11. Business Rules
A customer may have multiple accounts; an account belongs to one customer.
Each account may have multiple transactions and balance snapshots.
Each financial transaction must create two GL entries (Debit & Credit) unless exception-approved.
Fee charges must not be applied to closed or inactive accounts.
KYC must be reviewed before the next_due_date.
Account status should always reflect the latest status event.
12. Non-Functional Requirements
Performance: Transaction and balance updates must be near real time.
Scalability: Must support millions of customers and accounts.
Security: Role-based access, PII data masking.
Auditability: Full traceability from GL entries to original transactions.
Availability: 24/7 system availability with maintenance windows.
Data Quality: Referential integrity and data completeness checks.
13. Assumptions & Dependencies
Upstream channels provide clean customer and transaction data.
KYC classification rules are provided by Compliance.
GL mapping tables are maintained by Finance.
Real-time transaction feeds are available for balance updates.
14. Risks
Poor data quality may impact reconciliation.
Missing KYC reviews may cause regulatory penalties.
Incorrect GL mapping may cause financial misstatements.
High transaction volumes may impact system performance.
15. Acceptance Criteria
All core entities (Customer, Account, Transaction, Balances, Fees, GL) are implemented.
Full data lineage is documented.
Reporting outputs reconcile with legacy systems.
GL entries balance for every transaction and fee event.
KYC & account status workflows validated by Compliance & Ops.

conceptual.rag_context_used:
Business concept: Customer. Canonical ER entity: CUSTOMER. Definition: Individual or organisation holding a banking relationship with the bank. Business usage: Represents the party that owns accounts and is subject to onboarding, servicing, and compliance processes. Notes: Top-level business entity in Core Banking.
Business concept: Customer Contact. Canonical ER entity: CUSTOMER_CONTACT. Definition: Customer communication and address details. Business usage: Stores contactability attributes used for statements, alerts, servicing, and regulatory communication. Notes: Separated from CUSTOMER in normalized design to avoid repeating contact fields.
Business concept: KYC Profile. Canonical ER entity: KYC_PROFILE. Definition: Know Your Customer compliance profile maintained for a customer. Business usage: Tracks KYC completion, risk rating, and review cycle for compliance and regulatory control. Notes: Compliance-focused entity linked to CUSTOMER.
Business concept: Account. Canonical ER entity: ACCOUNT. Definition: Financial record representing customer funds or liabilities. Business usage: Central Core Banking entity through which balances, transactions, fees, and lifecycle events are managed. Notes: Acts as the anchor entity in the Core Banking ER model.
Business concept: Account Balance. Canonical ER entity: ACCOUNT_BALANCE. Definition: Snapshot of account financial position at a point in time. Business usage: Used to represent ledger and available balances for customer channels, finance, and reconciliation. Notes: Often stored as periodic snapshots or intraday balance history.
Business concept: Transaction. Canonical ER entity: TRANSACTION. Definition: Debit or credit movement of funds on an account. Business usage: Represents operational money movement across channels, payments, transfers, withdrawals, and deposits. Notes: High-volume transactional entity.
Business concept: Account Status Event. Canonical ER entity: ACCOUNT_STATUS_EVENT. Definition: Recorded change in operational state of an account. Business usage: Captures events such as open, frozen, dormant, and closed status changes. Notes: Lifecycle tracking entity for accounts.
Business concept: Fee Charge. Canonical ER entity: FEE_CHARGE. Definition: Fee or charge applied to an account. Business usage: Represents service charges such as ATM fee, maintenance fee, penalty fee, or transaction fee. Notes: Operational fee assessment entity.
Business concept: GL Posting. Canonical ER entity: GL_POSTING. Definition: Debit or credit entry posted to the General Ledger. Business usage: Represents accounting impact of transactions and fees into the finance ledger. Notes: Finance-facing accounting entity derived from operational events.
Business concept: GL Account. Canonical ER entity: GL_ACCOUNT. Definition: General Ledger account used for financial classification. Business usage: Represents accounting buckets such as customer deposits, fee income, cash, and suspense accounts. Notes: Reference or master entity used in accounting postings.
Business concept: Normalization. Canonical ER entity: ER Modeling Principle. Definition: Data modeling approach that separates repeating or dependent data into related entities. Business usage: Used in operational and canonical models to reduce redundancy and improve consistency. Notes: In this Core Banking ER, CUSTOMER, CUSTOMER_CONTACT, and KYC_PROFILE are separated in normalized design.
Business concept: De-normalization. Canonical ER entity: Reporting or Analytics View. Definition: Data modeling approach that combines multiple related entities for easier reporting or analytics. Business usage: Commonly used in reporting, analytical marts, and data products. Notes: Useful for CUSTOMER_ACCOUNT_TXN style reporting views.
Business concept: Master Data. Canonical ER entity: CUSTOMER / ACCOUNT / KYC_PROFILE / GL_ACCOUNT. Definition: Relatively stable business data reused across multiple processes. Business usage: Examples include customer, account, KYC profile, and GL account records. Notes: Supports enterprise consistency across domains.
Business concept: Transactional Data. Canonical ER entity: TRANSACTION / ACCOUNT_BALANCE / FEE_CHARGE. Definition: Event-based data that changes frequently and records business activity. Business usage: Examples include transactions, balance snapshots, fee charges, and status events. Notes: Typically high-volume and time-sensitive.
Entity profile: CUSTOMER. Business terms: Customer. Identifiers and reference attributes: customer_id means Unique identifier assigned to a customer. Typical business attributes: first_name means Given name of the customer; last_name means Family name of the customer; customer_type means Segment or type of customer; date_of_birth means Date of birth for an individual customer. Notes and examples: customer_id: Stable business key or surrogate key in enterprise models.; first_name: Synthetic in sample data.; last_name: Synthetic in sample data.; customer_type: Retail / SME / Corporate.; date_of_birth: Needed mainly for retail and compliance use cases.
Entity profile: CUSTOMER_CONTACT. Business terms: Customer Contact. Identifiers and reference attributes: contact_id means Unique identifier for a customer contact record; customer_id means Customer to whom the contact details belong. Typical business attributes: mobile_number means Primary mobile number used for customer communication; email_address means Email address used for alerts and servicing; address_line_1 means Primary street or address line; city means City of residence or correspondence; postal_code means Postal or ZIP code. Notes and examples: customer_id: Links the contact record back to the customer.; mobile_number: Used for alerts and OTP-style contact.; email_address: Used for statements and notifications.
Entity profile: KYC_PROFILE. Business terms: KYC Profile. Identifiers and reference attributes: kyc_profile_id means Unique identifier for the KYC profile; customer_id means Customer to whom the KYC profile belongs. Typical business attributes: kyc_status means Current KYC completion state; risk_rating means Compliance risk rating associated with the customer; review_due_date means Date by which the next KYC review is required. Notes and examples: kyc_status: Pending / Completed / Expired.; risk_rating: Low / Medium / High.
Entity profile: ACCOUNT. Business terms: Account. Identifiers and reference attributes: account_id means Unique identifier for an account; customer_id means Customer who owns the account. Typical business attributes: account_type means Type of banking account; currency means ISO currency in which the account operates; account_status means Current operating status of account. Notes and examples: account_id: Central link to balances and transactions.; customer_id: Links account to customer.; account_type: CASA / TD / OD etc.; currency: GBP / USD / EUR / INR etc.; account_status: Open / Frozen / Dormant / Closed.
Entity profile: ACCOUNT_BALANCE. Business terms: Account Balance. Identifiers and reference attributes: balance_id means Unique identifier for an account balance snapshot; account_id means Account for which the balance was recorded. Typical business attributes: balance_date means Date or timestamp of the balance snapshot; ledger_balance means Official book balance; available_balance means Spendable or withdrawable balance. Notes and examples: available_balance: Important for channel and servicing scenarios.; balance_date: Can be end of day or intraday.
Entity profile: TRANSACTION. Business terms: Transaction. Identifiers and reference attributes: transaction_id means Unique identifier for a transaction; account_id means Account on which the transaction occurred. Typical business attributes: transaction_date means Date and time of the transaction; transaction_type means Classification of transaction; amount means Monetary amount moved; channel means Originating channel for the transaction. Notes and examples: transaction_type: Credit / Debit / Transfer / Withdrawal / Deposit.; channel: Branch / ATM / Mobile / Internet / API.
Entity profile: ACCOUNT_STATUS_EVENT. Business terms: Account Status Event. Identifiers and reference attributes: status_event_id means Unique identifier for an account status event; account_id means Account affected by the status event. Typical business attributes: status_code means New status or status event code; event_timestamp means Time at which the status event occurred; event_reason means Business reason for the status event. Notes and examples: status_code: Opened / Frozen / Dormant / Closed.
Entity profile: FEE_CHARGE. Business terms: Fee Charge. Identifiers and reference attributes: fee_charge_id means Unique identifier for the fee charge; account_id means Account to which the fee was applied. Typical business attributes: fee_type means Category of fee charged; charge_amount means Monetary value of the fee; charge_date means Date on which the fee was applied. Notes and examples: fee_type: ATM Fee / Maintenance Fee / Penalty Fee.
Entity profile: GL_POSTING. Business terms: GL Posting. Identifiers and reference attributes: gl_posting_id means Unique identifier for the general ledger posting; transaction_id means Transaction that triggered the posting; gl_account_id means General ledger account used for the posting. Typical business attributes: posting_date means Date of accounting recognition; debit_credit_indicator means Whether the entry is debit or credit; posting_amount means Monetary amount posted to the ledger. Notes and examples: debit_credit_indicator: D or C.
Entity profile: GL_ACCOUNT. Business terms: GL Account. Identifiers and reference attributes: gl_account_id means Unique identifier for a general ledger account. Typical business attributes: gl_account_code means Accounting classification code; gl_account_name means Human-readable ledger name; gl_account_type means Type of ledger account. Notes and examples: gl_account_type: Asset / Liability / Income / Expense.

conceptual.conceptual_summary: The Core Banking Conceptual Data Model establishes a unified view of banking operations, linking Customers to their Accounts, KYC profiles, and Contact details. Accounts are central, managing balances, transactions, fee charges, and status lifecycle events. All monetary activities (transactions) are recorded and lead to corresponding General Ledger Postings, which are classified against specific GL Accounts. This model supports consistent data management for customer servicing, financial reporting, and compliance.
conceptual.diagram_description: The conceptual diagram illustrates Customer as the central entity, linked to KYC Profile, Customer Contact details, and Accounts. Each Account branches out to capture its lifecycle through Status Events, financial state via Account Balances, operational activities through Transactions, and service charges via Fee Charges. Transactions are a primary source for GL Postings, which in turn are categorized by GL Accounts. The model highlights the foundational relationships within core banking operations.

Business Requirements Document (BRD)
Core Banking Domain Data & Functional Requirements
1. Document Purpose
This BRD outlines the business requirements for the Core Banking domain covering customer onboarding, account lifecycle management, transactions processing, account balances, fees & charges, KYC compliance, and GL postings. The document provides a unified business view leveraging the supplied Core Banking ER diagram to ensure standardized data, seamless processing, and accurate financial reporting.
2. Business Background
Banks operate critical core systems to handle customer information, accounts, balances, transactions, fees, and ledger postings. Current systems often involve multiple legacy modules, manual reconciliations, inconsistent account status updates, and delays in GL posting visibility. A unified and governed data/functional view is essential for operational efficiency, regulatory compliance, and audit readiness.
3. Business Objectives
Establish a standardized Core Banking data model aligned with the bank's operational workflow.
Centralize customer, account, balance, transaction, fee, and GL data.
Ensure accurate, real-time and historical visibility of all customer and account activities.
Support regulatory, financial, and reconciliation reporting.
Reduce manual dependencies and improve process transparency.
4. Scope
In Scope:
Customer master & contact management
Account creation, modification, status lifecycle tracking
Capturing and storing financial transactions
Maintaining ledger and available balances
Fee computation and fee assessment
KYC profiling and periodic review tracking
General Ledger (GL) postings derived from transactions and fees
Out of Scope:
Loan origination workflows
Card systems
Wealth management or investment products
CRM segmentation logic beyond core identifiers
Treasury, market risk, or payments switching
5. Stakeholders
Retail Banking Operations
Customer Service & Branch Users
Finance & GL/Accounting Teams
Compliance & KYC Teams
Risk & Monitoring Teams
Data Engineering / Core Banking IT
Audit & Regulatory Reporting
6. Current State Challenges
Customer data inconsistencies across channels
Multiple representations of account status without event history
Manual KYC review tracking
Lack of unified view of transactions and balances
Delayed fee and GL posting visibility
High dependency on manual reconciliations
7. Future State Requirements
A unified data store linking customers, accounts, KYC, balances, transactions, and GL entries.
Event-driven account status lifecycle tracking.
Automated generation of GL postings for all monetary transactions and fee charges.
Real-time or near real-time balance updates.
Standardized customer and account attributes.
Full audit trail for all monetary and non-monetary activities.

System shall store core customer attributes including name, gender, DOB, and segment.

System shall not allow transactions on closed or restricted accounts.

System shall update available balance and ledger balance in real time.
Each financial transaction shall create corresponding GL postings.
8.4 Balance Management
System shall maintain ledger and available balances for each account.
System shall maintain a timestamp for each balance snapshot.

8.5 Fees & Charges
System shall store fee definitions and transaction-level fee charges.
System shall apply fees based on fee type (maintenance, penalty, service fee, etc.).

model_json.rag_context_used:
Business concept: Customer. Canonical ER entity: CUSTOMER. Definition: Individual or organisation holding a banking relationship with the bank. Business usage: Represents the party that owns accounts and is subject to onboarding, servicing, and compliance processes. Notes: Top-level business entity in Core Banking.
Business concept: Customer Contact. Canonical ER entity: CUSTOMER_CONTACT. Definition: Customer communication and address details. Business usage: Stores contactability attributes used for statements, alerts, servicing, and regulatory communication. Notes: Separated from CUSTOMER in normalized design to avoid repeating contact fields.
Business concept: KYC Profile. Canonical ER entity: KYC_PROFILE. Definition: Know Your Customer compliance profile maintained for a customer. Business usage: Tracks KYC completion, risk rating, and review cycle for compliance and regulatory control. Notes: Compliance-focused entity linked to CUSTOMER.
Business concept: Account. Canonical ER entity: ACCOUNT. Definition: Financial record representing customer funds or liabilities. Business usage: Central Core Banking entity through which balances, transactions, fees, and lifecycle events are managed. Notes: Acts as the anchor entity in the Core Banking ER model.
Business concept: Account Balance. Canonical ER entity: ACCOUNT_BALANCE. Definition: Snapshot of account financial position at a point in time. Business usage: Used to represent ledger and available balances for customer channels, finance, and reconciliation. Notes: Often stored as periodic snapshots or intraday balance history.
Business concept: Transaction. Canonical ER entity: TRANSACTION. Definition: Debit or credit movement of funds on an account. Business usage: Represents operational money movement across channels, payments, transfers, withdrawals, and deposits. Notes: High-volume transactional entity.
Business concept: Account Status Event. Canonical ER entity: ACCOUNT_STATUS_EVENT. Definition: Recorded change in operational state of an account. Business usage: Captures events such as open, frozen, dormant, and closed status changes. Notes: Lifecycle tracking entity for accounts.
Business concept: Fee Charge. Canonical ER entity: FEE_CHARGE. Definition: Fee or charge applied to an account. Business usage: Represents service charges such as ATM fee, maintenance fee, penalty fee, or transaction fee. Notes: Operational fee assessment entity.
Business concept: GL Posting. Canonical ER entity: GL_POSTING. Definition: Debit or credit entry posted to the General Ledger. Business usage: Represents accounting impact of transactions and fees into the finance ledger. Notes: Finance-facing accounting entity derived from operational events.
Business concept: GL Account. Canonical ER entity: GL_ACCOUNT. Definition: General Ledger account used for financial classification. Business usage: Represents accounting buckets such as customer deposits, fee income, cash, and suspense accounts. Notes: Reference or master entity used in accounting postings.
Business concept: Normalization. Canonical ER entity: ER Modeling Principle. Definition: Data modeling approach that separates repeating or dependent data into related entities. Business usage: Used in operational and canonical models to reduce redundancy and improve consistency. Notes: In this Core Banking ER, CUSTOMER, CUSTOMER_CONTACT, and KYC_PROFILE are separated in normalized design.
Business concept: De-normalization. Canonical ER entity: Reporting or Analytics View. Definition: Data modeling approach that combines multiple related entities for easier reporting or analytics. Business usage: Commonly used in reporting, analytical marts, and data products. Notes: Useful for CUSTOMER_ACCOUNT_TXN style reporting views.
Business concept: Master Data. Canonical ER entity: CUSTOMER / ACCOUNT / KYC_PROFILE / GL_ACCOUNT. Definition: Relatively stable business data reused across multiple processes. Business usage: Examples include customer, account, KYC profile, and GL account records. Notes: Supports enterprise consistency across domains.
Business concept: Transactional Data. Canonical ER entity: TRANSACTION / ACCOUNT_BALANCE / FEE_CHARGE. Definition: Event-based data that changes frequently and records business activity. Business usage: Examples include transactions, balance snapshots, fee charges, and status events. Notes: Typically high-volume and time-sensitive.
Entity profile: CUSTOMER. Business terms: Customer. Identifiers and reference attributes: customer_id means Unique identifier assigned to a customer. Typical business attributes: first_name means Given name of the customer; last_name means Family name of the customer; customer_type means Segment or type of customer; date_of_birth means Date of birth for an individual customer. Notes and examples: customer_id: Stable business key or surrogate key in enterprise models.; first_name: Synthetic in sample data.; last_name: Synthetic in sample data.; customer_type: Retail / SME / Corporate.; date_of_birth: Needed mainly for retail and compliance use cases.
Entity profile: CUSTOMER_CONTACT. Business terms: Customer Contact. Identifiers and reference attributes: contact_id means Unique identifier for a customer contact record; customer_id means Customer to whom the contact details belong. Typical business attributes: mobile_number means Primary mobile number used for customer communication; email_address means Email address used for alerts and servicing; address_line_1 means Primary street or address line; city means City of residence or correspondence; postal_code means Postal or ZIP code. Notes and examples: customer_id: Links the contact record back to the customer.; mobile_number: Used for alerts and OTP-style contact.; email_address: Used for statements and notifications.
Entity profile: KYC_PROFILE. Business terms: KYC Profile. Identifiers and reference attributes: kyc_profile_id means Unique identifier for the KYC profile; customer_id means Customer to whom the KYC profile belongs. Typical business attributes: kyc_status means Current KYC completion state; risk_rating means Compliance risk rating associated with the customer; review_due_date means Date by which the next KYC review is required. Notes and examples: kyc_status: Pending / Completed / Expired.; risk_rating: Low / Medium / High.
Entity profile: ACCOUNT. Business terms: Account. Identifiers and reference attributes: account_id means Unique identifier for an account; customer_id means Customer who owns the account. Typical business attributes: account_type means Type of banking account; currency means ISO currency in which the account operates; account_status means Current operating status of account. Notes and examples: account_id: Central link to balances and transactions.; customer_id: Links account to customer.; account_type: CASA / TD / OD etc.; currency: GBP / USD / EUR / INR etc.; account_status: Open / Frozen / Dormant / Closed.
Entity profile: ACCOUNT_BALANCE. Business terms: Account Balance. Identifiers and reference attributes: balance_id means Unique identifier for an account balance snapshot; account_id means Account for which the balance was recorded. Typical business attributes: balance_date means Date or timestamp of the balance snapshot; ledger_balance means Official book balance; available_balance means Spendable or withdrawable balance. Notes and examples: available_balance: Important for channel and servicing scenarios.; balance_date: Can be end of day or intraday.
Entity profile: TRANSACTION. Business terms: Transaction. Identifiers and reference attributes: transaction_id means Unique identifier for a transaction; account_id means Account on which the transaction occurred. Typical business attributes: transaction_date means Date and time of the transaction; transaction_type means Classification of transaction; amount means Monetary amount moved; channel means Originating channel for the transaction. Notes and examples: transaction_type: Credit / Debit / Transfer / Withdrawal / Deposit.; channel: Branch / ATM / Mobile / Internet / API.
Entity profile: ACCOUNT_STATUS_EVENT. Business terms: Account Status Event. Identifiers and reference attributes: status_event_id means Unique identifier for an account status event; account_id means Account affected by the status event. Typical business attributes: status_code means New status or status event code; event_timestamp means Time at which the status event occurred; event_reason means Business reason for the status event. Notes and examples: status_code: Opened / Frozen / Dormant / Closed.
Entity profile: FEE_CHARGE. Business terms: Fee Charge. Identifiers and reference attributes: fee_charge_id means Unique identifier for the fee charge; account_id means Account to which the fee was applied. Typical business attributes: fee_type means Category of fee charged; charge_amount means Monetary value of the fee; charge_date means Date on which the fee was applied. Notes and examples: fee_type: ATM Fee / Maintenance Fee / Penalty Fee.
Entity profile: GL_POSTING. Business terms: GL Posting. Identifiers and reference attributes: gl_posting_id means Unique identifier for the general ledger posting; transaction_id means Transaction that triggered the posting; gl_account_id means General ledger account used for the posting. Typical business attributes: posting_date means Date of accounting recognition; debit_credit_indicator means Whether the entry is debit or credit; posting_amount means Monetary amount posted to the ledger. Notes and examples: debit_credit_indicator: D or C.
Entity profile: GL_ACCOUNT. Business terms: GL Account. Identifiers and reference attributes: gl_account_id means Unique identifier for a general ledger account. Typical business attributes: gl_account_code means Accounting classification code; gl_account_name means Human-readable ledger name; gl_account_type means Type of ledger account. Notes and examples: gl_account_type: Asset / Liability / Income / Expense.

Accounts are central, managing balances, transactions, fee charges, and status lifecycle events. All monetary activities (transactions) are recorded and lead to corresponding General Ledger Postings, which are classified against specific GL Accounts. This model supports consistent data management for customer servicing, financial reporting, and compliance.
model_json.diagram_description: The conceptual diagram illustrates Customer as the central entity, linked to KYC Profile, Customer Contact details, and Accounts. Transactions are a primary source for GL Postings, which in turn are categorized by GL Accounts. The model highlights the foundational relationships within core banking operations.

System shall restrict customer onboarding unless minimum KYC criteria are met.
8.2 Account Management
System shall support creation of multiple account types (savings, current, deposit, etc.).
System shall track account currency, status, and ownership.
System shall store full account lifecycle status events (open, freeze, suspend, close, etc.).
System shall not allow transactions on closed or restricted accounts.
8.3 Transactions Processing
System shall store every transaction with timestamp, type, amount, and channel.
System shall support credit, debit, transfer, and adjustment transactions.
System shall update available balance and ledger balance in real time.
Each financial transaction shall create corresponding GL postings.
8.4 Balance Management
System shall maintain ledger and available balances for each account.
System shall maintain a timestamp for each balance snapshot.
System shall prevent transactions that exceed available balance where applicable.
8.5 Fees & Charges
System shall store fee definitions and transaction-level fee charges.
System shall apply fees based on fee type (maintenance, penalty, service fee, etc.).
System shall generate GL postings for all fee charges.
8.6 GL Posting & Accounting
System shall maintain mapping between transaction types and GL codes.
Every transaction and fee charge shall produce debit/credit GL entries.
System shall prevent unbalanced GL postings.
9. Reporting Requirements
Customer 360 view including customer profile, contact, KYC, and accounts.
Account 360 view including balance, status, transactions, and fees.
Account lifecycle status events reporting.
Daily transaction summaries by channel, type, and amount.
Fee revenue reporting by fee type and account.
GL trial balance alignment and exception monitoring.
KYC due-for-review reporting.
10. Data Requirements (Based on ER Diagram)
CUSTOMER: customer_id (PK), first_name, last_name, gender, date_of_birth, segment
CUSTOMER_CONTACT: customer_id (FK), mobile_number, email_id, address, city, country
ACCOUNT: account_id (PK), customer_id (FK), account_type, currency, account_status
ACCOUNT_STATUS_EVENT: status_event_id (PK), account_id (FK), old_status, new_status, change_timestamp
ACCOUNT_BALANCE: balance_id (PK), account_id (FK), as_of_timestamp, ledger_balance, available_balance
TRANSACTION: transaction_id (PK), account_id (FK), timestamp, type, amount, channel
FEE_CHARGE: fee_id (PK), account_id (FK), fee_type, fee_amount, assessment_date
KYC_PROFILE: kyc_id (PK), customer_id (FK), kyc_status, risk_rating, last_review_date, next_due_date
GL_ACCOUNT: gl_account_code (PK), gl_account_name, gl_type
GL_POSTING: gl_posting_id (PK), transaction_id (FK), gl_account_code (FK), dc_indicator, amount
11. Business Rules
A customer may have multiple accounts; an account belongs to one customer.
Each account may have multiple transactions and balance snapshots.
Each financial transaction must create two GL entries (Debit & Credit) unless exception-approved.
Fee charges must not be applied to closed or inactive accounts.
KYC must be reviewed before the next_due_date.
Account status should always reflect the latest status event.
12. Non-Functional Requirements
Performance: Transaction and balance updates must be near real time.
Scalability: Must support millions of customers and accounts.
Security: Role-based access, PII data masking.
Auditability: Full traceability from GL entries to original transactions.
Availability: 24/7 system availability with maintenance windows.
Data Quality: Referential integrity and data completeness checks.
13. Assumptions & Dependencies
Upstream channels provide clean customer and transaction data.
KYC classification rules are provided by Compliance.
GL mapping tables are maintained by Finance.
Real-time transaction feeds are available for balance updates.
14. Risks
Poor data quality may impact reconciliation.
Missing KYC reviews may cause regulatory penalties.
Incorrect GL mapping may cause financial misstatements.
High transaction volumes may impact system performance.
15. Acceptance Criteria
All core entities (Customer, Account, Transaction, Balances, Fees, GL) are implemented.
Full data lineage is documented.
Reporting outputs reconcile with legacy systems.
GL entries balance for every transaction and fee event.
KYC & account status workflows validated by Compliance & Ops.
conceptual.rag_context_used:
Business concept: Customer. Canonical ER entity: CUSTOMER. Definition: Individual or organisation holding a banking relationship with the bank. Business usage: Represents the party that owns accounts and is subject to onboarding, servicing, and compliance processes. Notes: Top-level business entity in Core Banking.
Business concept: Customer Contact. Canonical ER entity: CUSTOMER_CONTACT. Definition: Customer communication and address details. Business usage: Stores contactability attributes used for statements, alerts, servicing, and regulatory communication. Notes: Separated from CUSTOMER in normalized design to avoid repeating contact fields.
Business concept: KYC Profile. Canonical ER entity: KYC_PROFILE. Definition: Know Your Customer compliance profile maintained for a customer. Business usage: Tracks KYC completion, risk rating, and review cycle for compliance and regulatory control. Notes: Compliance-focused entity linked to CUSTOMER.
Business concept: Account. Canonical ER entity: ACCOUNT. Definition: Financial record representing customer funds or liabilities. Business usage: Central Core Banking entity through which balances, transactions, fees, and lifecycle events are managed. Notes: Acts as the anchor entity in the Core Banking ER model.
Business concept: Account Balance. Canonical ER entity: ACCOUNT_BALANCE. Definition: Snapshot of account financial position at a point in time. Business usage: Used to represent ledger and available balances for customer channels, finance, and reconciliation. Notes: Often stored as periodic snapshots or intraday balance history.
Business concept: Transaction. Canonical ER entity: TRANSACTION. Definition: Debit or credit movement of funds on an account. Business usage: Represents operational money movement across channels, payments, transfers, withdrawals, and deposits. Notes: High-volume transactional entity.
Business concept: Account Status Event. Canonical ER entity: ACCOUNT_STATUS_EVENT. Definition: Recorded change in operational state of an account. Business usage: Captures events such as open, frozen, dormant, and closed status changes. Notes: Lifecycle tracking entity for accounts.
Business concept: Fee Charge. Canonical ER entity: FEE_CHARGE. Definition: Fee or charge applied to an account. Business usage: Represents service charges such as ATM fee, maintenance fee, penalty fee, or transaction fee. Notes: Operational fee assessment entity.
Business concept: GL Posting. Canonical ER entity: GL_POSTING. Definition: Debit or credit entry posted to the General Ledger. Business usage: Represents accounting impact of transactions and fees into the finance ledger. Notes: Finance-facing accounting entity derived from operational events.
Business concept: GL Account. Canonical ER entity: GL_ACCOUNT. Definition: General Ledger account used for financial classification. Business usage: Represents accounting buckets such as customer deposits, fee income, cash, and suspense accounts. Notes: Reference or master entity used in accounting postings.
Business concept: Normalization. Canonical ER entity: ER Modeling Principle. Definition: Data modeling approach that separates repeating or dependent data into related entities. Business usage: Used in operational and canonical models to reduce redundancy and improve consistency. Notes: In this Core Banking ER, CUSTOMER, CUSTOMER_CONTACT, and KYC_PROFILE are separated in normalized design.
Business concept: De-normalization. Canonical ER entity: Reporting or Analytics View. Definition: Data modeling approach that combines multiple related entities for easier reporting or analytics. Business usage: Commonly used in reporting, analytical marts, and data products. Notes: Useful for CUSTOMER_ACCOUNT_TXN style reporting views.
Business concept: Master Data. Canonical ER entity: CUSTOMER / ACCOUNT / KYC_PROFILE / GL_ACCOUNT. Definition: Relatively stable business data reused across multiple processes. Business usage: Examples include customer, account, KYC profile, and GL account records. Notes: Supports enterprise consistency across domains.
Business concept: Transactional Data. Canonical ER entity: TRANSACTION / ACCOUNT_BALANCE / FEE_CHARGE. Definition: Event-based data that changes frequently and records business activity. Business usage: Examples include transactions, balance snapshots, fee charges, and status events. Notes: Typically high-volume and time-sensitive.
Entity profile: CUSTOMER. Business terms: Customer. Identifiers and reference attributes: customer_id means Unique identifier assigned to a customer. Typical business attributes: first_name means Given name of the customer; last_name means Family name of the customer; customer_type means Segment or type of customer; date_of_birth means Date of birth for an individual customer. Notes and examples: customer_id: Stable business key or surrogate key in enterprise models.; first_name: Synthetic in sample data.; last_name: Synthetic in sample data.; customer_type: Retail / SME / Corporate.; date_of_birth: Needed mainly for retail and compliance use cases.
Entity profile: CUSTOMER_CONTACT. Business terms: Customer Contact. Identifiers and reference attributes: contact_id means Unique identifier for a customer contact record; customer_id means Customer to whom the contact details belong. Typical business attributes: mobile_number means Primary mobile number used for customer communication; email_address means Email address used for alerts and servicing; address_line_1 means Primary street or address line; city means City of residence or correspondence; postal_code means Postal or ZIP code. Notes and examples: customer_id: Links the contact record back to the customer.; mobile_number: Used for alerts and OTP-style contact.; email_address: Used for statements and notifications.
Entity profile: KYC_PROFILE. Business terms: KYC Profile. Identifiers and reference attributes: kyc_profile_id means Unique identifier for the KYC profile; customer_id means Customer to whom the KYC profile belongs. Typical business attributes: kyc_status means Current KYC completion state; risk_rating means Compliance risk rating associated with the customer; review_due_date means Date by which the next KYC review is required. Notes and examples: kyc_status: Pending / Completed / Expired.; risk_rating: Low / Medium / High.
Entity profile: ACCOUNT. Business terms: Account. Identifiers and reference attributes: account_id means Unique identifier for an account; customer_id means Customer who owns the account. Typical business attributes: account_type means Type of banking account; currency means ISO currency in which the account operates; account_status means Current operating status of account. Notes and examples: account_id: Central link to balances and transactions.; customer_id: Links account to customer.; account_type: CASA / TD / OD etc.; currency: GBP / USD / EUR / INR etc.; account_status: Open / Frozen / Dormant / Closed.
Entity profile: ACCOUNT_BALANCE. Business terms: Account Balance. Identifiers and reference attributes: balance_id means Unique identifier for an account balance snapshot; account_id means Account for which the balance was recorded. Typical business attributes: balance_date means Date or timestamp of the balance snapshot; ledger_balance means Official book balance; available_balance means Spendable or withdrawable balance. Notes and examples: available_balance: Important for channel and servicing scenarios.; balance_date: Can be end of day or intraday.
Entity profile: TRANSACTION. Business terms: Transaction. Identifiers and reference attributes: transaction_id means Unique identifier for a transaction; account_id means Account on which the transaction occurred. Typical business attributes: transaction_date means Date and time of the transaction; transaction_type means Classification of transaction; amount means Monetary amount moved; channel means Originating channel for the transaction. Notes and examples: transaction_type: Credit / Debit / Transfer / Withdrawal / Deposit.; channel: Branch / ATM / Mobile / Internet / API.
Entity profile: ACCOUNT_STATUS_EVENT. Business terms: Account Status Event. Identifiers and reference attributes: status_event_id means Unique identifier for an account status event; account_id means Account affected by the status event. Typical business attributes: status_code means New status or status event code; event_timestamp means Time at which the status event occurred; event_reason means Business reason for the status event. Notes and examples: status_code: Opened / Frozen / Dormant / Closed.
Entity profile: FEE_CHARGE. Business terms: Fee Charge. Identifiers and reference attributes: fee_charge_id means Unique identifier for the fee charge; account_id means Account to which the fee was applied. Typical business attributes: fee_type means Category of fee charged; charge_amount means Monetary value of the fee; charge_date means Date on which the fee was applied. Notes and examples: fee_type: ATM Fee / Maintenance Fee / Penalty Fee.
Entity profile: GL_POSTING. Business terms: GL Posting. Identifiers and reference attributes: gl_posting_id means Unique identifier for the general ledger posting; transaction_id means Transaction that triggered the posting; gl_account_id means General ledger account used for the posting. Typical business attributes: posting_date means Date of accounting recognition; debit_credit_indicator means Whether the entry is debit or credit; posting_amount means Monetary amount posted to the ledger. Notes and examples: debit_credit_indicator: D or C.
Entity profile: GL_ACCOUNT. Business terms: GL Account. Identifiers and reference attributes: gl_account_id means Unique identifier for a general ledger account. Typical business attributes: gl_account_code means Accounting classification code; gl_account_name means Human-readable ledger name; gl_account_type means Type of ledger account. Notes and examples: gl_account_type: Asset / Liability / Income / Expense.

conceptual.conceptual_summary: This conceptual data model for core banking illustrates the key business entities and their relationships. It captures Customer information, including contact details and KYC profiles, and links them to their Accounts. Accounts are central to tracking balances, transactions, status lifecycle events, and associated fee charges. All financial events, including transactions and fees, ultimately lead to General Ledger Postings, which are classified against specific GL Accounts. The model supports a unified view for operational efficiency, compliance, and financial reporting.
conceptual.diagram_description: The diagram consists of ten interconnected entities. 'Customer' is linked to 'Customer Contact', 'KYC Profile', and 'Account'. 'Account' is a central entity, linked to 'Customer', and also to 'Account Balance', 'Transaction', 'Account Status Event', and 'Fee Charge'. Both 'Transaction' and 'Fee Charge' are linked to 'GL Posting'. Finally, 'GL Posting' is linked to 'GL Account'. All entities are interconnected without any isolated components.

relationships.description: A Customer may have multiple Customer Contact details. Implemented via Customer_ID foreign key in CUSTOMER_CONTACT.
A Customer has one KYC Profile. Implemented via Customer_ID foreign key in KYC_PROFILE.
A Customer may own multiple Accounts. Implemented via Customer_ID foreign key in ACCOUNT.
An Account has multiple Account Balance snapshots over time. Implemented via Account_ID foreign key in ACCOUNT_BALANCE.
An Account has multiple Transactions. Implemented via Account_ID foreign key in TRANSACTION.
An Account has a history of multiple Account Status Events. Implemented via Account_ID foreign key in ACCOUNT_STATUS_EVENT.
An Account may incur multiple Fee Charges. Implemented via Account_ID foreign key in FEE_CHARGE.
Each Transaction results in one or more GL Postings. Implemented via a nullable Transaction_ID foreign key in GL_POSTING.
Each Fee Charge results in one or more GL Postings. Implemented via a nullable Fee_Charge_ID foreign key in GL_POSTING.
A GL Account is associated with multiple GL Postings. Implemented via GL_Account_ID foreign key in GL_POSTING.

normalization_notes: A GL_POSTING record must originate from either a TRANSACTION or a FEE_CHARGE, but not both. This implies that exactly one of the foreign keys (Transaction_ID or Fee_Charge_ID) must be populated for each GL_POSTING record. This is a business rule at the logical level.
Unique business identifiers have been added: Account_Number (ACCOUNT table), GL_Account_Code (GL_ACCOUNT table), and Transaction_Reference_Number (TRANSACTION table).

The Account_Status in the ACCOUNT table represents the current operational state and should ideally be derived from or kept consistent with the latest Account_Status_Event based on Event_Timestamp from the ACCOUNT_STATUS_EVENT table.

For CUSTOMER_CONTACT, while individual contact attributes like Mobile_Number, Email_Address, Address_Line, City, and Postal_Code are nullable, a complete customer contact record should ideally have at least one valid communication method. This is a business rule.

ddl:
CREATE TABLE CUSTOMER (
  Customer_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  First_Name VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,
  Last_Name VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,
  Customer_Type VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,
  Date_Of_Birth DATE NOT NULL,
  Customer_Status VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,
  Created_Timestamp TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
  Last_Updated_Timestamp TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
  CONSTRAINT pk_CUSTOMER PRIMARY KEY (Customer_ID)
);

CREATE TABLE CUSTOMER_CONTACT (
  Customer_Contact_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  Customer_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  Mobile_Number VARCHAR(255) NULL,
  Email_Address VARCHAR(255) NULL,
  Address_Line VARCHAR(500) NULL,
  City VARCHAR(255) NULL,
  Postal_Code VARCHAR(20) NULL,
  Contact_Type VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,
  Is_Primary_Contact BOOLEAN NOT NULL,
  Created_Timestamp TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
  Last_Updated_Timestamp TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
  CONSTRAINT pk_CUSTOMER_CONTACT PRIMARY KEY (Customer_Contact_ID),
  CONSTRAINT fk_CUSTOMER_CONTACT_Customer_ID FOREIGN KEY (Customer_ID) REFERENCES CUSTOMER (Customer_ID)
);

CREATE TABLE KYC_PROFILE (
  KYC_Profile_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  Customer_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  KYC_Status VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,
  Risk_Rating VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,
  Review_Due_Date DATE NOT NULL,
  KYC_Last_Review_Date DATE NULL,
  Created_Timestamp TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
  Last_Updated_Timestamp TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
  CONSTRAINT pk_KYC_PROFILE PRIMARY KEY (KYC_Profile_ID),
  CONSTRAINT fk_KYC_PROFILE_Customer_ID FOREIGN KEY (Customer_ID) REFERENCES CUSTOMER (Customer_ID)
);

CREATE TABLE ACCOUNT (
  Account_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  Customer_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  Account_Number VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,
  Account_Type VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,
  Currency_Code VARCHAR(3) NOT NULL,
  Account_Status VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,
  Opening_Date DATE NOT NULL,
  Closing_Date DATE NULL,
  Created_Timestamp TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
  Last_Updated_Timestamp TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
  CONSTRAINT pk_ACCOUNT PRIMARY KEY (Account_ID),
  CONSTRAINT fk_ACCOUNT_Customer_ID FOREIGN KEY (Customer_ID) REFERENCES CUSTOMER (Customer_ID)
);

CREATE TABLE ACCOUNT_BALANCE (
  Account_Balance_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  Account_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  Balance_Timestamp TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
  Ledger_Balance_Amount DECIMAL(18, 4) NOT NULL,
  Available_Balance_Amount DECIMAL(18, 4) NOT NULL,
  Balance_Type VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,
  Created_Timestamp TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
  CONSTRAINT pk_ACCOUNT_BALANCE PRIMARY KEY (Account_Balance_ID),
  CONSTRAINT fk_ACCOUNT_BALANCE_Account_ID FOREIGN KEY (Account_ID) REFERENCES ACCOUNT (Account_ID)
);

CREATE TABLE TRANSACTION (
  Transaction_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  Account_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  Transaction_Reference_Number VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,
  Transaction_Timestamp TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
  Transaction_Type VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,
  Transaction_Amount DECIMAL(18, 4) NOT NULL,
  Channel VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,
  Description VARCHAR(500) NULL,
  Value_Date DATE NOT NULL,
  Created_Timestamp TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
  CONSTRAINT pk_TRANSACTION PRIMARY KEY (Transaction_ID),
  CONSTRAINT fk_TRANSACTION_Account_ID FOREIGN KEY (Account_ID) REFERENCES ACCOUNT (Account_ID)
);

CREATE TABLE ACCOUNT_STATUS_EVENT (
  Account_Status_Event_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  Account_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  Account_New_Status VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,
  Account_Old_Status VARCHAR(255) NULL,
  Event_Timestamp TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
  Event_Reason VARCHAR(500) NULL,
  Created_Timestamp TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
  CONSTRAINT pk_ACCOUNT_STATUS_EVENT PRIMARY KEY (Account_Status_Event_ID),
  CONSTRAINT fk_ACCOUNT_STATUS_EVENT_Account_ID FOREIGN KEY (Account_ID) REFERENCES ACCOUNT (Account_ID)
);

CREATE TABLE FEE_CHARGE (
  Fee_Charge_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  Account_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  Fee_Type VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,
  Charge_Amount DECIMAL(18, 4) NOT NULL,
  Assessment_Date DATE NOT NULL,
  Charge_Status VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,
  Description VARCHAR(500) NULL,
  Created_Timestamp TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
  Last_Updated_Timestamp TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
  CONSTRAINT pk_FEE_CHARGE PRIMARY KEY (Fee_Charge_ID),
  CONSTRAINT fk_FEE_CHARGE_Account_ID FOREIGN KEY (Account_ID) REFERENCES ACCOUNT (Account_ID)
);

CREATE TABLE GL_POSTING (
  GL_Posting_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  GL_Account_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  Transaction_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  Fee_Charge_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  Posting_Date DATE NOT NULL,
  Debit_Credit_Indicator VARCHAR(10) NOT NULL,
  Posting_Amount DECIMAL(18, 4) NOT NULL,
  Posting_Reference VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,
  Description VARCHAR(500) NULL,
  Created_Timestamp TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
  CONSTRAINT pk_GL_POSTING PRIMARY KEY (GL_Posting_ID),
  CONSTRAINT fk_GL_POSTING_GL_Account_ID FOREIGN KEY (GL_Account_ID) REFERENCES GL_ACCOUNT (GL_Account_ID),
  CONSTRAINT fk_GL_POSTING_Transaction_ID FOREIGN KEY (Transaction_ID) REFERENCES TRANSACTION (Transaction_ID),
  CONSTRAINT fk_GL_POSTING_Fee_Charge_ID FOREIGN KEY (Fee_Charge_ID) REFERENCES FEE_CHARGE (Fee_Charge_ID)
);

CREATE TABLE GL_ACCOUNT (
  GL_Account_ID BIGINT NOT NULL,
  GL_Account_Code VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,
  GL_Account_Name VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,
  GL_Account_Type VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,
  Account_Category VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,
  Created_Timestamp TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
  Last_Updated_Timestamp TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
  CONSTRAINT pk_GL_ACCOUNT PRIMARY KEY (GL_Account_ID)
);
CREATE INDEX IX_CUSTOMER_CONTACT_CUSTOMER_ID ON CUSTOMER_CONTACT (Customer_ID);
CREATE INDEX IX_KYC_PROFILE_CUSTOMER_ID ON KYC_PROFILE (Customer_ID);
CREATE INDEX IX_ACCOUNT_CUSTOMER_ID ON ACCOUNT (Customer_ID);
CREATE INDEX IX_ACCOUNT_BALANCE_ACCOUNT_ID ON ACCOUNT_BALANCE (Account_ID);
CREATE INDEX IX_TRANSACTION_ACCOUNT_ID ON TRANSACTION (Account_ID);
CREATE INDEX IX_ACCOUNT_STATUS_EVENT_ACCOUNT_ID ON ACCOUNT_STATUS_EVENT (Account_ID);
CREATE INDEX IX_FEE_CHARGE_ACCOUNT_ID ON FEE_CHARGE (Account_ID);
CREATE INDEX IX_GL_POSTING_GL_ACCOUNT_ID ON GL_POSTING (GL_Account_ID);
CREATE INDEX IX_GL_POSTING_TRANSACTION_ID ON GL_POSTING (Transaction_ID);
CREATE INDEX IX_GL_POSTING_FEE_CHARGE_ID ON GL_POSTING (Fee_Charge_ID);

BRD Document Content:
Business Requirements Document (BRD)
Core Banking Domain Data & Functional Requirements
1. Document Purpose
This BRD outlines the business requirements for the Core Banking domain covering customer onboarding, account lifecycle management, transactions processing, account balances, fees & charges, KYC compliance, and GL postings. The document provides a unified business view leveraging the supplied Core Banking ER diagram to ensure standardized data, seamless processing, and accurate financial reporting.
2. Business Background
Banks operate critical core systems to handle customer information, accounts, balances, transactions, fees, and ledger postings. Current systems often involve multiple legacy modules, manual reconciliations, inconsistent account status updates, and delays in GL posting visibility. A unified and governed data/functional view is essential for operational efficiency, regulatory compliance, and audit readiness.
3. Business Objectives
Establish a standardized Core Banking data model aligned with the bank's operational workflow.
Centralize customer, account, balance, transaction, fee, and GL data.
Ensure accurate, real-time and historical visibility of all customer and account activities.
Support regulatory, financial, and reconciliation reporting.
Reduce manual dependencies and improve process transparency.
4. Scope
In Scope:
Customer master & contact management
Account creation, modification, status lifecycle tracking
Capturing and storing financial transactions
Maintaining ledger and available balances
Fee computation and fee assessment
KYC profiling and periodic review tracking
General Ledger (GL) postings derived from transactions and fees
Out of Scope:
Loan origination workflows
Card systems
Wealth management or investment products
CRM segmentation logic beyond core identifiers
Treasury, market risk, or payments switching
5. Stakeholders
Retail Banking Operations
Customer Service & Branch Users
Finance & GL/Accounting Teams
Compliance & KYC Teams
Risk & Monitoring Teams
Data Engineering / Core Banking IT
Audit & Regulatory Reporting
6. Current State Challenges
Customer data inconsistencies across channels
Multiple representations of account status without event history
Manual KYC review tracking
Lack of unified view of transactions and balances
Delayed fee and GL posting visibility
High dependency on manual reconciliations
7. Future State Requirements
A unified data store linking customers, accounts, KYC, balances, transactions, and GL entries.
Event-driven account status lifecycle tracking.
Automated generation of GL postings for all monetary transactions and fee charges.
Real-time or near real-time balance updates.
Standardized customer and account attributes.
Full audit trail for all monetary and non-monetary activities.
8. Functional Requirements
8.1 Customer & KYC
System shall store core customer attributes including name, gender, DOB, and segment.
System shall store multiple customer contact details including phone, email, and address.
System shall maintain KYC profile including status, risk rating, last review and next review date.
System shall restrict customer onboarding unless minimum KYC criteria are met.
8.2 Account Management
System shall support creation of multiple account types (savings, current, deposit, etc.).
System shall track account currency, status, and ownership.
System shall store full account lifecycle status events (open, freeze, suspend, close, etc.).
System shall not allow transactions on closed or restricted accounts.
8.3 Transactions Processing
System shall store every transaction with timestamp, type, amount, and channel.
System shall support credit, debit, transfer, and adjustment transactions.
System shall update available balance and ledger balance in real time.
Each financial transaction shall create corresponding GL postings.
8.4 Balance Management
System shall maintain ledger and available balances for each account.
System shall maintain a timestamp for each balance snapshot.
System shall prevent transactions that exceed available balance where applicable.
8.5 Fees & Charges
System shall store fee definitions and transaction-level fee charges.
System shall apply fees based on fee type (maintenance, penalty, service fee, etc.).
System shall generate GL postings for all fee charges.
8.6 GL Posting & Accounting
System shall maintain mapping between transaction types and GL codes.
Every transaction and fee charge shall produce debit/credit GL entries.
System shall prevent unbalanced GL postings.
9. Reporting Requirements
Customer 360 view including customer profile, contact, KYC, and accounts.
Account 360 view including balance, status, transactions, and fees.
Account lifecycle status events reporting.
Daily transaction summaries by channel, type, and amount.
Fee revenue reporting by fee type and account.
GL trial balance alignment and exception monitoring.
KYC due-for-review reporting.
10. Data Requirements (Based on ER Diagram)
CUSTOMER: customer_id (PK), first_name, last_name, gender, date_of_birth, segment
CUSTOMER_CONTACT: customer_id (FK), mobile_number, email_id, address, city, country
ACCOUNT: account_id (PK), customer_id (FK), account_type, currency, account_status
ACCOUNT_STATUS_EVENT: status_event_id (PK), account_id (FK), old_status, new_status, change_timestamp
ACCOUNT_BALANCE: balance_id (PK), account_id (FK), as_of_timestamp, ledger_balance, available_balance
TRANSACTION: transaction_id (PK), account_id (FK), timestamp, type, amount, channel
FEE_CHARGE: fee_id (PK), account_id (FK), fee_type, fee_amount, assessment_date
KYC_PROFILE: kyc_id (PK), customer_id (FK), kyc_status, risk_rating, last_review_date, next_due_date
GL_ACCOUNT: gl_account_code (PK), gl_account_name, gl_type
GL_POSTING: gl_posting_id (PK), transaction_id (FK), gl_account_code (FK), dc_indicator, amount
11. Business Rules
A customer may have multiple accounts; an account belongs to one customer.
Each account may have multiple transactions and balance snapshots.
Each financial transaction must create two GL entries (Debit & Credit) unless exception-approved.
Fee charges must not be applied to closed or inactive accounts.
KYC must be reviewed before the next_due_date.
Account status should always reflect the latest status event.
12. Non-Functional Requirements
Performance: Transaction and balance updates must be near real time.
Scalability: Must support millions of customers and accounts.
Security: Role-based access, PII data masking.
Auditability: Full traceability from GL entries to original transactions.
Availability: 24/7 system availability with maintenance windows.
Data Quality: Referential integrity and data completeness checks.
13. Assumptions & Dependencies
Upstream channels provide clean customer and transaction data.
KYC classification rules are provided by Compliance.
GL mapping tables are maintained by Finance.
Real-time transaction feeds are available for balance updates.
14. Risks
Poor data quality may impact reconciliation.
Missing KYC reviews may cause regulatory penalties.
Incorrect GL mapping may cause financial misstatements.
High transaction volumes may impact system performance.
15. Acceptance Criteria
All core entities (Customer, Account, Transaction, Balances, Fees, GL) are implemented.
Full data lineage is documented.
Reporting outputs reconcile with legacy systems.
GL entries balance for every transaction and fee event.
KYC & account status workflows validated by Compliance & Ops.

model_json.rag_context_used:
Business concept: Customer. Canonical ER entity: CUSTOMER. Definition: Individual or organisation holding a banking relationship with the bank. Business usage: Represents the party that owns accounts and is subject to onboarding, servicing, and compliance processes. Notes: Top-level business entity in Core Banking.
Business concept: Customer Contact. Canonical ER entity: CUSTOMER_CONTACT. Definition: Customer communication and address details. Business usage: Stores contactability attributes used for statements, alerts, servicing, and regulatory communication. Notes: Separated from CUSTOMER in normalized design to avoid repeating contact fields.
Business concept: KYC Profile. Canonical ER entity: KYC_PROFILE. Definition: Know Your Customer compliance profile maintained for a customer. Business usage: Tracks KYC completion, risk rating, and review cycle for compliance and regulatory control. Notes: Compliance-focused entity linked to CUSTOMER.
Business concept: Account. Canonical ER entity: ACCOUNT. Definition: Financial record representing customer funds or liabilities. Business usage: Central Core Banking entity through which balances, transactions, fees, and lifecycle events are managed. Notes: Acts as the anchor entity in the Core Banking ER model.
Business concept: Account Balance. Canonical ER entity: ACCOUNT_BALANCE. Definition: Snapshot of account financial position at a point in time. Business usage: Used to represent ledger and available balances for customer channels, finance, and reconciliation. Notes: Often stored as periodic snapshots or intraday balance history.
Business concept: Transaction. Canonical ER entity: TRANSACTION. Definition: Debit or credit movement of funds on an account. Business usage: Represents operational money movement across channels, payments, transfers, withdrawals, and deposits. Notes: High-volume transactional entity.
Business concept: Account Status Event. Canonical ER entity: ACCOUNT_STATUS_EVENT. Definition: Recorded change in operational state of an account. Business usage: Captures events such as open, frozen, dormant, and closed status changes. Notes: Lifecycle tracking entity for accounts.
Business concept: Fee Charge. Canonical ER entity: FEE_CHARGE. Definition: Fee or charge applied to an account. Business usage: Represents service charges such as ATM fee, maintenance fee, penalty fee, or transaction fee. Notes: Operational fee assessment entity.
Business concept: GL Posting. Canonical ER entity: GL_POSTING. Definition: Debit or credit entry posted to the General Ledger. Business usage: Represents accounting impact of transactions and fees into the finance ledger. Notes: Finance-facing accounting entity derived from operational events.
Business concept: GL Account. Canonical ER entity: GL_ACCOUNT. Definition: General Ledger account used for financial classification. Business usage: Represents accounting buckets such as customer deposits, fee income, cash, and suspense accounts. Notes: Reference or master entity used in accounting postings.
Business concept: Normalization. Canonical ER entity: ER Modeling Principle. Definition: Data modeling approach that separates repeating or dependent data into related entities. Business usage: Used in operational and canonical models to reduce redundancy and improve consistency. Notes: In this Core Banking ER, CUSTOMER, CUSTOMER_CONTACT, and KYC_PROFILE are separated in normalized design.
Business concept: De-normalization. Canonical ER entity: Reporting or Analytics View. Definition: Data modeling approach that combines multiple related entities for easier reporting or analytics. Business usage: Commonly used in reporting, analytical marts, and data products. Notes: Useful for CUSTOMER_ACCOUNT_TXN style reporting views.
Business concept: Master Data. Canonical ER entity: CUSTOMER / ACCOUNT / KYC_PROFILE / GL_ACCOUNT. Definition: Relatively stable business data reused across multiple processes. Business usage: Examples include customer, account, KYC profile, and GL account records. Notes: Supports enterprise consistency across domains.
Business concept: Transactional Data. Canonical ER entity: TRANSACTION / ACCOUNT_BALANCE / FEE_CHARGE. Definition: Event-based data that changes frequently and records business activity. Business usage: Examples include transactions, balance snapshots, fee charges, and status events. Notes: Typically high-volume and time-sensitive.
Entity profile: CUSTOMER. Business terms: Customer. Identifiers and reference attributes: customer_id means Unique identifier assigned to a customer. Typical business attributes: first_name means Given name of the customer; last_name means Family name of the customer; customer_type means Segment or type of customer; date_of_birth means Date of birth for an individual customer. Notes and examples: customer_id: Stable business key or surrogate key in enterprise models.; first_name: Synthetic in sample data.; last_name: Synthetic in sample data.; customer_type: Retail / SME / Corporate.; date_of_birth: Needed mainly for retail and compliance use cases.
Entity profile: CUSTOMER_CONTACT. Business terms: Customer Contact. Identifiers and reference attributes: contact_id means Unique identifier for a customer contact record; customer_id means Customer to whom the contact details belong. Typical business attributes: mobile_number means Primary mobile number used for customer communication; email_address means Email address used for alerts and servicing; address_line_1 means Primary street or address line; city means City of residence or correspondence; postal_code means Postal or ZIP code. Notes and examples: customer_id: Links the contact record back to the customer.; mobile_number: Used for alerts and OTP-style contact.; email_address: Used for statements and notifications.
Entity profile: KYC_PROFILE. Business terms: KYC Profile. Identifiers and reference attributes: kyc_profile_id means Unique identifier for the KYC profile; customer_id means Customer to whom the KYC profile belongs. Typical business attributes: kyc_status means Current KYC completion state; risk_rating means Compliance risk rating associated with the customer; review_due_date means Date by which the next KYC review is required. Notes and examples: kyc_status: Pending / Completed / Expired.; risk_rating: Low / Medium / High.
Entity profile: ACCOUNT. Business terms: Account. Identifiers and reference attributes: account_id means Unique identifier for an account; customer_id means Customer who owns the account. Typical business attributes: account_type means Type of banking account; currency means ISO currency in which the account operates; account_status means Current operating status of account. Notes and examples: account_id: Central link to balances and transactions.; customer_id: Links account to customer.; account_type: CASA / TD / OD etc.; currency: GBP / USD / EUR / INR etc.; account_status: Open / Frozen / Dormant / Closed.
Entity profile: ACCOUNT_BALANCE. Business terms: Account Balance. Identifiers and reference attributes: balance_id means Unique identifier for an account balance snapshot; account_id means Account for which the balance was recorded. Typical business attributes: balance_date means Date or timestamp of the balance snapshot; ledger_balance means Official book balance; available_balance means Spendable or withdrawable balance. Notes and examples: available_balance: Important for channel and servicing scenarios.; balance_date: Can be end of day or intraday.
Entity profile: TRANSACTION. Business terms: Transaction. Identifiers and reference attributes: transaction_id means Unique identifier for a transaction; account_id means Account on which the transaction occurred. Typical business attributes: transaction_date means Date and time of the transaction; transaction_type means Classification of transaction; amount means Monetary amount moved; channel means Originating channel for the transaction. Notes and examples: transaction_type: Credit / Debit / Transfer / Withdrawal / Deposit.; channel: Branch / ATM / Mobile / Internet / API.
Entity profile: ACCOUNT_STATUS_EVENT. Business terms: Account Status Event. Identifiers and reference attributes: status_event_id means Unique identifier for an account status event; account_id means Account affected by the status event. Typical business attributes: status_code means New status or status event code; event_timestamp means Time at which the status event occurred; event_reason means Business reason for the status event. Notes and examples: status_code: Opened / Frozen / Dormant / Closed.
Entity profile: FEE_CHARGE. Business terms: Fee Charge. Identifiers and reference attributes: fee_charge_id means Unique identifier for the fee charge; account_id means Account to which the fee was applied. Typical business attributes: fee_type means Category of fee charged; charge_amount means Monetary value of the fee; charge_date means Date on which the fee was applied. Notes and examples: fee_type: ATM Fee / Maintenance Fee / Penalty Fee.
Entity profile: GL_POSTING. Business terms: GL Posting. Identifiers and reference attributes: gl_posting_id means Unique identifier for the general ledger posting; transaction_id means Transaction that triggered the posting; gl_account_id means General ledger account used for the posting. Typical business attributes: posting_date means Date of accounting recognition; debit_credit_indicator means Whether the entry is debit or credit; posting_amount means Monetary amount posted to the ledger. Notes and examples: debit_credit_indicator: D or C.
Entity profile: GL_ACCOUNT. Business terms: GL Account. Identifiers and reference attributes: gl_account_id means Unique identifier for a general ledger account. Typical business attributes: gl_account_code means Accounting classification code; gl_account_name means Human-readable ledger name; gl_account_type means Type of ledger account. Notes and examples: gl_account_type: Asset / Liability / Income / Expense.

model_json.conceptual_summary: This conceptual data model for core banking illustrates the key business entities and their relationships. It captures Customer information, including contact details and KYC profiles, and links them to their Accounts. Accounts are central to tracking balances, transactions, status lifecycle events, and associated fee charges. All financial events, including transactions and fees, ultimately lead to General Ledger Postings, which are classified against specific GL Accounts. The model supports a unified view for operational efficiency, compliance, and financial reporting.
model_json.diagram_description: The diagram consists of ten interconnected entities. 'Customer' is linked to 'Customer Contact', 'KYC Profile', and 'Account'. 'Account' is a central entity, linked to 'Customer', and also to 'Account Balance', 'Transaction', 'Account Status Event', and 'Fee Charge'. Both 'Transaction' and 'Fee Charge' are linked to 'GL Posting'. Finally, 'GL Posting' is linked to 'GL Account'. All entities are interconnected without any isolated components.

relationships.description: A Customer may have multiple Customer Contact details. Implemented via Customer_ID foreign key in CUSTOMER_CONTACT.
A Customer has one KYC Profile. Implemented via Customer_ID foreign key in KYC_PROFILE.
A Customer may own multiple Accounts. Implemented via Customer_ID foreign key in ACCOUNT.
An Account has multiple Account Balance snapshots over time. Implemented via Account_ID foreign key in ACCOUNT_BALANCE.
An Account has multiple Transactions. Implemented via Account_ID foreign key in TRANSACTION.
An Account has a history of multiple Account Status Events. Implemented via Account_ID foreign key in ACCOUNT_STATUS_EVENT.
An Account may incur multiple Fee Charges. Implemented via Account_ID foreign key in FEE_CHARGE.
Each Transaction results in one or more GL Postings. Implemented via a nullable Transaction_ID foreign key in GL_POSTING.
Each Fee Charge results in one or more GL Postings. Implemented via a nullable Fee_Charge_ID foreign key in GL_POSTING.
A GL Account is associated with multiple GL Postings. Implemented via GL_Account_ID foreign key in GL_POSTING.

normalization_notes: A GL_POSTING record must originate from either a TRANSACTION or a FEE_CHARGE, but not both. This implies that exactly one of the foreign keys (Transaction_ID or Fee_Charge_ID) must be populated for each GL_POSTING record. This is a business rule at the logical level.
Unique business identifiers have been added: Account_Number (ACCOUNT table), GL_Account_Code (GL_ACCOUNT table), and Transaction_Reference_Number (TRANSACTION table).

The Account_Status in the ACCOUNT table represents the current operational state and should ideally be derived from or kept consistent with the latest Account_Status_Event based on Event_Timestamp from the ACCOUNT_STATUS_EVENT table.

For CUSTOMER_CONTACT, while individual contact attributes like Mobile_Number, Email_Address, Address_Line, City, and Postal_Code are nullable, a complete customer contact record should ideally have at least one valid communication method. This is a business rule.
